raw_sales.title:
Lydia, Persian Imperial Coinage. Circa 450-330 B.C. AR
siglos. Bearded archer right in kneeling-running attitude, holding
spear and bow/Oblong incuse. 5.51 g. Babelon plate 86, 13. EF45
ICG. The relationship of silver to gold at this time was close to
13:1, as one unit of gold would be worth 13 units of silver. As I
write this, the ratio in 2002 is about 65:1! The ancient rate was
reduced still further, until 10:1 was established at the time of
the Philip II-Alexander III gold and silver issues minted in great
quantity. The relationship of bronze coinage was different, as it
was not a precious metal, and had a greater fiduciary than
intrinsic value. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100124)

raw_sales.title:
Siculo-Punic Coinage, "The Mint of the Camp." Circa 305-295
B.C. AR tetradrachm. Head right of young Melqart-Herakles wearing
lion skin/Horse's head left; behind, palm tree. 17.01 g. Jenkins
(SNR 57) 314 (same obverse die). EF. "The Mint (or People) of the Camp" is the most often seen
inscription on the coins of the Siculo-Punic class. The legend, in
Punic, is well-defined on this coin and can be read below the
horse's head on the reverse. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100026)

raw_sales.title:
Zeugitana, Carthage. Circa 3rd-2nd centuries B.C. Billon
tridrachm. Head left of Tanit wearing wreath of two corn ears and
leaf/Horse standing right with foreleg raised, head reverted; dot
in lower field. 8.71 g. SNG Cop 394. About EF for issue. The term "billon," or base silver, is used to characterize coins
composed of less than half their weight of pure silver. It is a sad
commentary on the earlier magnificent coins of Carthage that she
sank so low as to create poorly executed, carelessly struck coins
such as this, and in low grade metal. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100034)
raw_sales.raw_cert_text:

raw_sales.title:

unusual and provocative coin type. Other examples can be found in

raw_sales.title:
Kingdom of Macedon. Alexander III, the Great, 336-323 B.C.
AR tetradrachm minted at Sidon, c. 331-327 B.C. Head right of young
Herakles wearing lion skin/Zeus enthroned left, holding eagle and
scepter. 17.06 g. Newell (Dated Alexander Coinage of Sidon and Ake)
15, SNG Ashmolean 3067. AU50 ICG, lightly porous, wonderful
relief. Earlier, Sidon had been perhaps the most powerful city in
Phoenicia. During a devastating revolt, however, Sidon was
destroyed, and rebuilt to some extent about the time this coin was
struck. Since the military fortifications were not reconstructed,
it never again regained its former strength. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100048)

AR drachm minted at Sardes, c. 334-323 B.C. Head right of young
Herakles wearing lion skin/Zeus enthroned left, holding eagle and
scepter; monogram in left field. 4.25 g. Cf. Price 2576, Thompson
134 ff. AU58 ICG, boldly struck. The former capital of the Lydian monarchy, Sardes was probably
the first mint to be established by Alexander in all of
Asia. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100060)

raw_sales.title:
Kingdom of Macedon. Alexander III, the Great, 336-323 B.C.
AR drachm minted in Western Asia Minor, c. 323-280 B.C. Head right
of young Herakles wearing lion skin/Zeus enthroned left, holding
eagle and scepter; monogram in left field. 4.21 g. Price 2780, SNG
Cop 967. VF+, obverse die flaw. A complete suite of silver denominations minted by Alexander of
the standard type would include a dekadrachm, tetradrachm,
didrachm, drachm, hemidrachm and obol. The dekadrachm and didrachm
are particularly rare. (PCGS# 100063)

raw_sales.title:
Kingdom of Macedon. Philip III, 323-317 B.C. AR tetradrachm
minted in the East, probably Babylon, 323-317 B.C. Head right of
young Herakles wearing lion skin/Zeus enthroned left, holding eagle
and scepter; wheel in left field and two monograms. 16.68 g. Price
P189. Boldly struck VF, slightly porous with the obverse being
somewhat off-center. Philip III was a half-brother of Alexander the Great. Son of
Philip II and the dancer Philinna of Larissa, Philip III continued
coinage of Alexander's main types, sometimes retaining Alexander's
name, other times replacing it with his own, as with this coin. (PCGS# 100067)

raw_sales.title:
Macedon as a Roman Province. Aesillas as Quaestor, 93-88
B.C. and later. AR tetradrachm minted at Pella, c. 80-70 B.C. Head
right of Alexander the Great, monogram in front/Fiscus (money
chest), club of Herakles and quaestor's chair, all within laurel
wreath. 16.85 g. SNG Cop 1327. AU58 ICG, boldly struck and
choice. Aesillas was appointed during the Roman Republic as quaestor, or
financial magistrate, in charge of minting coins in northern
Greece. His symbols are the money chest (fiscus) and the quaestor's
chair (subsellium), both depicted on the reverse of this
charismatic coin. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100070)
raw_sales.raw_cert_text:

raw_sales.title:
Thrace, Maroneia. Circa 400-350 B.C. AR light-weight drachm.
Forepart of prancing horse left/Dotted square containing vine
branch with hanging cluster of grapes. 2.90 g. BMC 31. AU50
ICG. Homer mentions the excellent wine of this ancient city, of which
Apollo and Dionysos were favorite gods. In the Odyssey, it is
Maron, a son of Dionysos, who gives Odysseus the wine with which he
would later intoxicate Polyphemos. Maroneia was named from this son
of Dionysos, and she consistently used Dionysos, grape themes and
horses on her coinage. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100073)

generals, did likewise in Egypt. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100078)

raw_sales.title:
Kingdom of Thrace. Lysimachos, 323-281 B.C. AR drachm minted
at Colophon, 299-296 B.C. Head of young Herakles wearing lion
skin/Zeus enthroned left, holding eagle and scepter; in left field,
forepart of lion left over crescent; under throne, pentagram. 4.08
g. Thompson 127. AU58 ICG, lustrous and virtually Mint State. Coins proclaiming Lysimachos as king, but retaining the
Alexander the Great types (Herakles/Zeus), were the first coins to
be struck at the earlier established mints like Colophon. These are
quite scarce, and, when they approach such superb condition as the
above, are rarely encountered. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100080)

raw_sales.title:
Thessaly. The Thessalian League, 196-146 B.C. AR double
victoriatus minted c. 44-40 B.C. Head right of Zeus crowned with
oak leaves/Athena Itonia advancing right, holding shield and
brandishing spear; tripod in right field. 5.99 g. Dewing 1373. AU55
ICG, of late style. At this time (remember Julius Caesar died in 44 B.C.) Roman
Republican coinage was the currency most prevalent in Greece. The
double victoriatus was tariffed at 11/2 silver denarii and
circulated alongside the diverse coinage of the Republican
moneyers. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100084)

raw_sales.title:
Thessaly. The Perrhaebi, c. 400-334 B.C. AE 21. Veiled head
of Hera facing/Zeus standing left, holding thunderbolt and scepter.
SNG Cop 196. Overall VF but with scattered craters filled with
powdery green cancroid. While the firm that sold this coin to Dr. Seventko in 1991
described it as having "brown patina with red highlights," through
either poor storage, or, more likely, late 20th century harmful
techniques in cleaning (acids were probably used), this rare bronze
now has what we call "the bronze disease." It is called a disease
because the problem is known to spread quickly to other portions of
the coin, or even "jump" to other bronzes in a cabinet. There are
ways to halt this insidious metallurgical alteration and relieve
the surfaces of the coin; it can yet be saved for future
collectors. The procedure should be done by a conservator, and
usually involves heating the coin as part of the treatment. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100087)
raw_sales.raw_cert_text:

raw_sales.raw_cert_text:

right-angled skew pattern. 12.33 g. Dewing 1686, BMC 165. VF with

raw_sales.title:
Aigina. Circa 510-485 B.C. AR obol. Turtle with single row
of dots down the middle/Incuse square with dividing lines. 0.97 g.
Dewing 1670, SNG Delepierre 1530. About Fine, irregularly shaped
flan. As hoards are found and new scholarship promulgated, our
glimpses into ancient numismatics change. One of these parameters
is the question of dating Greek coins, especially archaic ones. If
catalogued in the 1960s, for example, these two "turtles" would be
dated considerably earlier. Through R. Holloway's excellent
research, published in the ANS Museum Notes 17 (1971) and Price and
Waggoner's Archaic Greek Silver Coinage: The "Asyut" Hoard, and
other scholars' work, we can be more confident in today's
perspective of early Greek coinage. (PCGS#
100096)

raw_sales.title:
C. Titinius, moneyer. 141 B.C. AR denarius minted at Rome.
Helmeted head right of Roma, "XVI" behind/Victory in biga right.
Rare. 4.01 g. Cr. 226/1, Syd. 445, Titinia 7. AU55 ICG, some light
scattered porosity, especially on the reverse. The denarius has now been retariffed at 16 asses, instead of 10
as earlier, hence "XVI" behind the obverse head of Roma; see T.
Buttrey's article in ANS Museum Notes 7 (1957). Michael
Crawford's corpus Roman Republican Coinage (the Cr. number)
is the latest major reference, but Sydenham numbers and Babelon's
organization by gens or family name will also be given. This system
of classification is based on the moneyer's main family name,
followed by a number indicating the chronology within that
particular family. Roman Silver Coins, Volume I, published
by Seaby, is an easy-to-use and readily available reference which
is based on the moneyer's gens. From this point, all Republican,
Imperatorial and Imperial coins can be assumed to be struck at Rome
unless otherwise indicated. Since most all issues were struck al
marco, weights are unnecessary and will be omitted from catalogue
entries. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100302)

raw_sales.title:
Q. Pomponius Musa, moneyer. 66 B.C. AR denarius. Laureate
head right of Apollo; behind, tuning-wrench of kithara/Calliope
standing right, playing kithara resting on column. Cr. 410/2a, Syd.
811, Pomponia 9. About VF, mark on Apollo's cheek. An allusion to the moneyer's cognomen "Musa" was chosen
in the reverse representations of the nine Muses, accompanied by
Hercules Musarum on one reverse type. Calliope was the Muse of Epic
Poetry. (PCGS# 100320)

raw_sales.title:
Q. Pomponius Musa, moneyer. 66 B.C. AR denarius. Laureate
head right of Apollo; behind, scepter/Melpomene standing facing,
holding club and mask. Cr. 410/4, Syd. 816, Pomponia 14. Choice
VF. All the nine "Muses" minted by Q. Pomponius Musa are quite
scarce save one. For some reason, the denarii bearing the reverse
with Erato, Muse of Erotic Poetry, are extremely rare and seldom
offered. On this coin, Melpomene, the Muse of Tragedy, is depicted,
holding her attributes. (PCGS#
100321)

raw_sales.title:
Julius Caesar, d. 44 B.C. Fourrée denarius. Diademed head
right of Apollo/Aeneas walking left, carrying palladium in
right hand and Anchises on left shoulder. Cf. Cr. 444/1 for
obverse, Cr. 458/1 for reverse. Fine+. An ancient counterfeit fabricated by combining an obverse type
of Sicinia 1 (original struck 49 B.C. at a mint moving with Pompey)
and a reverse type of Caesar's (original struck 47-46 B.C. in
Africa by Caesar). A fascinating hybrid fourrée! Fourrées are
plated coins, made by the fusion of a base metal core with a silver
overlay. Unitized by pressure, fourrées can be identified by their
copper core showing through, especially on the edges and pockmarks
inherent in the procedure. They are most often lighter in weight
than the originals, and have a cruder and softer appearance. (PCGS# 100345)

raw_sales.title:
Nero Claudius Drusus, d. 9 B.C. AE sestertius minted
posthumously by his son, the emperor Claudius, A.D. 50-54. Bare
head left of Nero Claudius Drusus/Claudius seated left amidst arms.
RIC 109 (under Claudius) [as Scarce], BMC 208. Nearly EF with
remarkable reverse detail, dark reddish brown patina. These sestertii, worth one-quarter of a silver denarius, were
struck from orichalcum, which is very similar in composition to
modern brass. When these coins were minted, then, they were of a
shiny brass color and must have been goldlike - a treat to behold!
Through their centuries in the soil, the AE or copper alloy coinage
has developed patination, highly variable in color and evenness.
The acidity or alkalinity of the burial spot has a great deal to do
with how the coin looks today. For example, the volcanic soil of
Sicily can impart a hard green or even blue patina (accent on the
first syllable is preferred), which is so magnificent and highly
prized. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100368)

raw_sales.title:
Nero, A.D. 54-68. Two orichalcum dupondii of Nero, both
minted at Lugdunum. One reverse is Victory flying left (Fine+), the
other shows Securitas seated right (Fine+/Good, porous). The dupondius was worth two asses, and normally exhibits an
obverse radiate or rayed portrait. On these two Seventko examples,
the emperor is laureate. Nero's copper-alloy coinage is complex,
utilizing both orichalcum and copper for the medium-sized
denominations. Engravers from Lugdunum often placed a globe at the
truncation of Nero's portrait to indicate place of minting. From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (Total: 2
coins) (PCGS# 100379)

raw_sales.title:
Vespasian, A.D. 69-79. AV aureus minted at Lugdunum, c. A.D.
70-71. Laureate head right of Vespasian/Victory advancing left,
holding wreath and palm. 7.22 g. RIC 295 [as Scarce], C. 597. Edge
shaved and test cut, otherwise VF. This coin is likely a commemorative issue of the Roman victory
of the Jewish War (A.D. 66-70). From the Collection of Dr. Joseph M. Seventko. (PCGS# 100391)

raw_sales.title:
Domitian, A.D. 81-96. AE sestertius minted A.D. 85. Laureate
bust right of Domitian with aegis/Domitian standing left, holding
spear; in front, German kneeling right, holding shield. RIC 258, C.
488. About VF, porous. There's something special about 1st century sestertii - their
realistic portraiture, commemorative reverses, imposing size; each
one has a unique character or look all its own. Is it sacrilege to
note that the German captive's portrait on this coin resembles
George Washington? (PCGS# 100406)

raw_sales.title:
Nerva, A.D. 96-98. AE sestertius minted A.D. 97. Laureate
head right of Nerva/Clasped hands holding legionary eagle standard
which rests on prow. RIC 96, C. 35. About VF with a pleasing
portrait, thin, brownish-green patina and struck slightly
off-center. Celebrating "the harmony of the armies," it was to be the armies
that would cause Nerva's downfall. The Praetorian Guard and
soldiers in general did not respect Nerva, partly for his probable
implication in the murder of Domitian, the previous emperor. It was
wise of Nerva to have adopted Trajan, the strong and enlightened
governor of Upper Germania, as his son and heir. (PCGS# 100411)
raw_sales.raw_cert_text:

Constantinople. Helmeted and cuirassed beardless bust facing,
holding globe surmounted by Victory and shield/Constantinopolis
seated facing, head right, holding spear and globus cruciger. 4.25
g. D.O. 4, Ratto 754. EF, snipped at edge. The practice of cutting or filing the edge of ancient gold coins
was common, rather like the disreputable method of shaking U.S.
gold coins in bags to retrieve the gold dust. Full-weight, no-wear
Byzantine solidi should weigh around 4.44-4.52 grams. When
purchasing any gold coin, I recommend first looking at the edges.
Any telltale flat, shiny marks? Insist on knowing the weight and
compare it to published specimens. Gold has always been valuable;
the issuing authority was almost always very careful, producing
coins of close-tolerance weight and fineness. (PCGS# 100715)

raw_sales.title:
Heraclius, A.D. 610-641. AV solidus minted at
Constantinople, A.D. 613-616. Facing busts of Heraclius and
Heraclius Constantine/Cross potent on three steps. 4.36. D.O. 8,
MIB 8a. EF. This joint-reign coinage, showing Heraclius and his infant son
Heraclius Constantine, was the first coinage of this type to be
minted extensively. It was used as a pattern for hundreds of years.
For examples, compare this with those issued by Constans II,
Justinian II (first reign), Leo IV, Constantine VII and Romanus
I. (PCGS# 100752)
